coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photo-Fenton degradation of dyes under visible light with recyclable γ-Fe2O3/CQDs: Catalyst preparation, performance and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Qian
Xue, Qian
Tan, Shengmei
Cheng, Zhiliang
Qi, Xueqiang
Yan, Chaoqun
coredata.dc:description: A low band gap and visible light-responsive heterogeneous Photo-Fenton catalyst of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs micron composite was prepared under the one-pot hydrothermal method. The Photo-Fenton degradation of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs towards dye solution of rhodamine B(RhB), methyl blue (MB), and methyl orange (MO) was studied comparatively with α-Fe2O3. The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs exhibited remarkable catalytic performance for various dyes and with a first-order rate (k) of 14 times higher than that of initial α-Fe2O3 with a low concentration of H2O2 of 0.049 mmol. L−1 and a wider pH range of 3.1–7.1. The microstructure of the compounds was observed by XRD, SEM, TEM, FT-IR, and XPS characterization results suggested that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs nanocomposite was formed through the stable Fe–O–C bonds, thus, the band gap decreased, and it is more favorable for the distance of holes and electrons. The free radical trapping experiment and EPR analysis indicated that •OH and 1O2 were the major active species during the typical photo-Fenton reaction. What's more, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs also exhibited good stability and magnetic properties. DFT conclusion shows that the mechanism of the potential determination step (PDS) on α-Fe2O3(220) is the cleavage of H2O2 with an energy barrier of only 0.08 eV, which is 0.54 eV lower than that of OH* on γ-Fe2O3(220). Thus it can be deemed that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs perform much higher catalytic activity for the dissociation of H2O2 than α-Fe2O3. This work gives a feasible and economical countermeasure of visible light Photo-Fenton dispose of dye wastewater with a recyclable magnetic γ-Fe2O3/CQDs micron catalyst.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photo-Fenton degradation of dyes under visible light with recyclable γ-Fe2O3/CQDs: Catalyst preparation, performance and mechanism insight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A low band gap and visible light-responsive heterogeneous Photo-Fenton catalyst of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs micron composite was prepared under the one-pot hydrothermal method. The Photo-Fenton degradation of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs towards dye solution of rhodamine B(RhB), methyl blue (MB), and methyl orange (MO) was studied comparatively with α-Fe2O3. The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs exhibited remarkable catalytic performance for various dyes and with a first-order rate (k) of 14 times higher than that of initial α-Fe2O3 with a low concentration of H2O2 of 0.049 mmol. L−1 and a wider pH range of 3.1–7.1. The microstructure of the compounds was observed by XRD, SEM, TEM, FT-IR, and XPS characterization results suggested that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs nanocomposite was formed through the stable Fe–O–C bonds, thus, the band gap decreased, and it is more favorable for the distance of holes and electrons. The free radical trapping experiment and EPR analysis indicated that •OH and 1O2 were the major active species during the typical photo-Fenton reaction. What's more, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs also exhibited good stability and magnetic properties. DFT conclusion shows that the mechanism of the potential determination step (PDS) on α-Fe2O3(220) is the cleavage of H2O2 with an energy barrier of only 0.08 eV, which is 0.54 eV lower than that of OH* on γ-Fe2O3(220). Thus it can be deemed that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs perform much higher catalytic activity for the dissociation of H2O2 than α-Fe2O3. This work gives a feasible and economical countermeasure of visible light Photo-Fenton dispose of dye wastewater with a recyclable magnetic γ-Fe2O3/CQDs micron catalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the fast development of civilization, the wastewater quantity is increasing gradually. Among them, printing and dyeing sewage has received widespread attention due to its toxicity, and being difficult to degrade (Dasgupta et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016). At the same time, discharging printing and dyeing wastewater into the natural water will affect the solubility of oxygen in water, the penetration of sunlight, and photosynthesis. There are many methods to treat dyeing wastewater. For example, physical adsorption, catalytic degradation, microbiological treatment (Yang et al., 2020), and advanced oxidation processes (Xiong et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2021) are applied to degrade dyeing wastewater water with good effect. Among them, photo-Fenton technology in advanced oxidation technology has become a key research technology in recent years due to its convenient technical realization and no secondary harm to the environment (Shanmugam et al., 2015; Khare et al., 2018; Wen et al., 2017). In addition, Photo-Fenton has the advantages of simple experimental conditions, short reaction time, and easy operation. Since the emergence of this method, it has received extensive attention.

Photo-Fenton combines with H2O2 to produce strong oxidizing •OH. Almost all organic pollutants in wastewater can be degraded. The main point of the development of this technology lies in the development of a high-efficiency photo-Fenton catalyst with good catalytic performance and easy separation from the liquid phase. Iron-based photo-Fenton catalysts are extensively concerned because of their good treatment effect, economical, and availability (Zeng et al., 2021). But it has shortcomings: (1) the microscale or nanoscale iron-based photo-Fenton catalyst has high H2O2 catalytic oxidation activity, but it is difficult to separate from wastewater and cannot be reused; (2) the visible light response range of iron-based photo-Fenton catalyst is narrow, and the activity of direct absorption and utilization of sunlight is low. For example, the gap of bandwidth of α-Fe2O3 is about 2.0 eV, which theoretically needs less than 620 nm to stimulate it to produce photo-Fenton activity. The energy of visible and infrared light sources with wavelengths higher than 620 nm makes it difficult to excite the catalytic activity of the catalyst. Therefore, designing a catalyst with a wide response in the visible light range has become a key factor in treating this type of material.

The CQDs are an important kind of nano-carbon material, owing to their large specific surface area, non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, good solubility in water, reliable chemical resistance, and unique up-conversion photoluminescence features, good application prospect (Das et al., 2018; Kulandaivalu et al., 2019). In particular, light-excited CQDs have good electron-accepting and electron-donating characteristics, which makes them ideal photosensitizers for constructing nanocomposite photo-catalysts with high efficiency of photo-generated charge separation (Gao et al., 2019a; Gao et al., 2019b; Han et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021a; Qian et al., 2018). Moreover, the excellent up-conversion photoluminescence properties of CQDs provide an effective method for generating 350–750 nm short-wave emission light under long-wavelength (near-infrared light, 800–1000 nm) excitation (Li et al., 2020). The up-converted emission light can in turn excite the modified photo-catalyst to generate light-excited charges, thereby expanding its photo-responsive area. Therefore, the combination of CQDs and photo-catalysts is considered an ideal strategy for obtaining excellent hybrid composite photo-catalysts. (Gu et al., 2018)prepared a new Carbon quantum dot doping α-FeOOH (CQD@FeOOH) material with plentiful active sites by using electrochemical etching and urea/ethanol solution co-mediated two-step method. The degradation rate constant (k) of CQD@FeOOH in the photocatalytic/Fenton system is 2.8 times that of the original α-FeOOH. Thus, the introduction of carbon quantum dots has a positive effect on photo-Fenton. Ying et al. (2020) prepared visible-light-driven photo-Fenton catalysts by coupling amorphous FeOOH-QDS with polymer photo-catalyst g-C3N4. In comparison with pristine g-C3N4 and mechanical mixture (FeOOH-g-C3N4), highly fragmented FeOOH-QDS mounted on g-C3N4 exhibited heightened degradation of MO and phenol in a visible-light system. Compared with pristine g-C3N4, the FeOOH/g-C3N4 mineralization efficiency is increased nearly 7 times, showing great potential in degrading refractory pollutants. Li et al. (Liu et al., 2017)synthesized a carbon quantum dot-polished magnetite catalyst (γ-Fe2O3/CQDs) by one-hydrothermal in the dark and visible-light system, the latter for Sulfamethoxazole (SMX) has a significant degradation effect. The CQDs can accelerate the in-situ reduction of the Fe3+ surface by iron dissolution. This process has a positive influence on the degradation of pollutants.

The method of compounding CQDs in a catalyst mainly adopts a two-step method and then uses a hydrothermal method or electrochemical etching method to compound CQDs and catalyst together, which has disadvantages such as uneven compound, cumbersome preparation steps, the large amount of use of organic agents and high preparation cost of catalyst. This study reports a green and friendly method prepared γ-Fe2O3/CQDs photo-Fenton catalyst for the first time. Different from the distributed preparation of CQDs and catalysts, this work mainly used glucose as a carbon source and FeCl3 as the iron source to prepare carbon quantum dots doped iron-based photo-Fenton material for the treatment of organic dye pollutants by one-pot hydrothermal way. This way has the advantages of simple operation, the high catalytic efficiency of the synthetic catalyst, and effective removal of dye pollutants, and the catalyst is highly magnetic and can be recovered well. In comparison with pristine α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, highly fragmented γ-Fe2O3/CQDs were heightened degradation of dye pollutants in the visible-light system. Therefore, it has a good application prospect.

Ferric chloride (FeCl3.6H2O, 99%), glucose (C6H12O6, 99%), sodium acetate (CH3COONa, 99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), rhodamine B(RhB, Mw = 479.01), methylene blue (MB, Mw = 319.86), methyl orange (MO, Mw = 327.33), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38%), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na, 99%), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 99%), tert-butanol (TBA, 99%), and sodium azide (NaN3, 99%) were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. All the chemicals were analytical grade, and the simulated dye wastewater was prepared with ultrapure water.

The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs photo-Fenton catalysts were prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal method with precursors of ferric chloride, and glucose by our own patented technology (Chinese invention patent No.: CN114768808A). The detailed preparation steps were described in Text S1.
The materials characterization is described in Text S2.

The specific amount of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst was weighed with an electronic balance and added to a conical flask containing a certain concentration of dye for adsorption experiments. The Erlenmeyer flask was placed in a shaker, plastic wrap was added to the mouth of the Erlenmeyer flask, samples were taken regularly, and the adsorption capacity was calculated by equations (1) and (2). The Langmuir and Freundlich equations were used to match the experimental data to determine the adsorption isotherm model of the iron-based-carbon quantum dot catalyst for dyes. The Langmuir isotherm equation is Eq. (3) and the Freundlich isotherm equation is Eq. (4):

where Qt and Qe (mg·g−1) are the adsorption amount of the catalyst at time t (min) and equilibrium time, respectively. C0 and Ce (mg·L−1) are the initial concentration and equilibrium concentration of the dye, respectively; V (mL) is the volume of the dye used; W (g) is the mass of theγ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst; Qm (mg·g−1) is the maximum adsorption amount of the monolayer molecule; KL (L·mg−1) is the adsorption process Heat of adsorption; KF and n are both Freundlich model constants representing adsorption capacity and adsorption strength, respectively.

The degradation effect of the catalyst was evaluated by the system of visible light irradiation with hydrogen peroxide using RhB, MO, and MB as simulated pollutants. First, 50 mg of the catalyst was diffused in a certain concentration of simulated dye wastewater (100 mL, 10 mg L−1). The suspension liquids were mixed in the dark to achieve adsorption equilibrium prior to visible light exposure. Under the visible light from a 300 W xenon lamp, 0.049 mol L−1 of H2O2 (30%) was added to the suspension of the reactor. Collect 4 mL reaction solution at regular intervals, take parallel samples at the same time, centrifuge, and filter to remove solids. Repeat each experiment for two groups. Absorption spectra of the dye solution were collected on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu) from 300 nm to 700 nm at ambient temperatures. Based on the Lambert-Beer law, the consistency of the remaining dye in the solution was evaluated by the intensity of the absorption peak at the highest intensity: A = A(λ)*b*c, where A is the measured absorption intensity and A (λ) is the wavelength-dependent Absorption coefficients, b and c are path length and analyze concentration.

To clarify the specific reaction mechanism between the catalyst and pollutants, TBA, EDTA-2Na, BQ, and sodium azide were used as capture agents to capture hydroxyl radicals, holes, superoxide radicals, and Single oxygen generated by the photo-Fenton process, respectively (Cui et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020). Except for adding the trapping agent, the rest of the test process is the same as the photo-Fenton step.
DFT computation details are described in Text S3.

The phase structure of the as-synthesized catalyst was first broken down by the X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). As illustrated in Fig. S3a, the catalyzed XRD pattern can be indexed to the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS NO: 330664), with two broad peaks corresponding to the (104) and (110) crystal planes, respectively.The clear diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ = 63.0° 57.4°, 43.4°, 35.7°,30.4°, which correspond to (440), (511), (400), (311), and (220) peaks of the newly synthesized material, consistent with the XRD patterns of magnetic γ-Fe2O3 (JCPDS card NO. 251402) (Li et al., 2019a,b). The results show that some of the Fe3+ in α-Fe2O3 was reduced to form magnetic ferric tetroxide under the condition of reducing (Cao et al., 2010) carbon source and suitable hydrothermal temperature. The Fe3O4 under hydrothermal oxidation conditions forms magnetic γ-Fe2O3(Gallagher et al., 1968; Cui et al., 2013). Not detected in the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs extra diffraction peak, it may (Biesinger et al., 2011) be associated with the ultrafine particle size of CQDs (Dong et al., 2021). Fig. S3b shows the FT-IR spectra of α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts. The stretching vibration at about 534 cm−1 is considered to be the Fe–O bond of α-Fe2O3, and the stretching vibration at about 576 cm−1 is considered to be the Fe–O bond of γ-Fe2O3. Stretching vibrations at 3431 cm−1 and 3438 cm−1 are thought to be –OH bonds while stretching at around 1407 cm−1 is thought to be C–C bonds (Liu et al., 2019). The signal peaks located at 1635 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 belong to the bending vibration of the H2O molecule H–OH(Dong et al., 2021). All over, the FT - IR and XRD spectrum, the crystalline form of the new micron material is transformed and the carbon is present in the material.

The XPS analysis further confirmed the composition of the elementary and chemical state of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. Fig. 1 a shows the survey XPS spectra of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts. The XPS spectra showed that C, O, and Fe elements were found in the new photo-Fenton catalytic material. Fig. 1b shows the C1s spectrum of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. The C1s XPS spectrum reveals multiple peaks at 288.81 eV, 286.67 eV, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to O–CO, C–O, and C–C (sp2) bonds (Wu et al., 2021b; Zhang et al., 2020), respectively. The carbon quantum dots are mainly made up of C, H, and O elements and contain functional groups such as carboxyl groups. All over, the existence of O–CO bonds proves the possible presence of carboxyl functional groups. In the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c), the peak values at 530.58 eV and 531.23 eV are allocated to Fe–O from γ-Fe2O3, respectively, while the Fe–O–C peak at 532.5 eV is connected with the peaks on the surface of CQDs related to oxygen-containing groups (Zhang et al., 2011). Fe 2p XPS spectra study found that two broad peaks of Fe 2p concentrated at 710.9 eV and 722.9 eV, which are Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p2/3, respectively (Wu et al., 2021a). It matched well with the oxidation state of Fe in γ-Fe2O3 (Fig. 1d). The above results further indicate that the material successfully composites carbon material.

The microstructure and morphology of powder α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts were compared by TEM and SEM analysis. As can be seen from Fig. S4a on the upper left, α-Fe2O3 has a relatively smooth surface, good particle dispersion, and a 200–400 nm pie in diameter. From the bottom of Fig. S4a, it can be observed that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs have a rough surface and uniform distribution, and it is filled with amounts of nanoparticles, showing a spherical structure with a particle diameter of 1μm–2μm. According to the morphology of the original γ-Fe2O3 is about 300 nm spherical particles (Prasannamedha et al., 2022), the size of the complex is larger. The increase in particle diameter of the composites may be due to the successful recombination of CQDs. The EDS elemental mapping shows the distribution of different elements. The mass fraction of C is about 14% and iron is 57 %. According to Figs. S4c and C, O, and Fe are evenly distributed in the catalyst. This further confirms the successful introduction of CQDs.

Fig. 2 a records the UV–Vis absorbance spectra (with an inset)of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs composites and α-Fe2O3 micron-particles. Both samples showed a broadband 19 of 533 nm (Wang et al., 2017). Compared with α-Fe2O3 Submicron material, as can be seen in γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, composite materials have continuous absorption bands in the scope of 550–800 nm, which indicates that they possibly have higher photocatalytic activity for the target reaction. After Kubelka–Munk function is transformed, can be roughly known as α-Fe2O3, γ-Fe2O3and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs band-gap energy are 2.5 eV,2.2 eV (Ji et al., 2022), and 1.87eV. The result shows that CQDs can effectively reduce bandgap energy, which is beneficial to improve the efficiency of electron-hole separation. Photoluminescence spectra are generated by charge carrier recombination, which helps to understand the behavior of electrons and holes during separation and recombination as photo-catalysts. The PL analysis was accustomed to further investigate the separation and recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 2b α-Fe2O3 exhibits a weak photoluminescence signal, which could be attributed to its low visible light response, as the addition of CQDs was able to heighten the visible light responsiveness of the composite and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs exhibited excellent optical absorption intensity at 377 nm in the UV–visible region, which indicating CQDs can effectively promote electron transfer and inhibits electron-hole recombination (Duan et al., 2020). This also provides a valid basis for the photo-Fenton reaction. Look further at its microscopic form (Fig. 2c). Based on the Fourier transform, the lattice of α-Fe2O3 distance are 0.27 nm, 0.25 nm, and 0.21 nm correspond to (104), (110), and (024) planes respectively. There are shown in Fig. 2d–g the lattice spacing of the composite catalyst is 0.22 nm, 0.25 nm, and 0.312 nm, Matching the (100) plane of the carbon quantum dot (Li et al., 2012) and the (311) and (220) plane of the γ-Fe2O3, respectively. The above results fully demonstrate the successful preparation of composite materials.

To further explore the adsorption capacity of the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst, the effects of different catalysts and different pollutant concentrations were investigated by catalyst adsorption experiments. It can be observed from Fig. S6a that the adsorption property of the catalyst is affected by the concentration of pollutants, and the adsorption amount can achieve 40 mg g−1. The molar ratios of iron and carbon are (1:0, 1:2, 1:4), which are denoted as α-Fe2O3/CQDsx for convenience, where x is the number of moles of carbon. At the same time, by comparing the adsorption performance of different catalysts, it can be observed from Fig. S6b that the adsorption capacity of catalyst doped with quantum dots is significantly improved, indicating that the doping of quantum dots can excellently improve the adsorption performance of catalysts.

To further find the reasons for the improved adsorption capacity of the catalyst, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the material were detected by N2 isothermal adsorption-desorption isothermal curve, and the consequences are reached in Fig. S5 and Table 1 . As shown in Fig. S5, α-Fe2O3 shows type Ⅳ isotherm, H4 mesoporous hysteresis loop, with typical mesoporous structure, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs shows type Ⅳ N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, H3 mesoporous hysteresis loop. In addition, it is not difficult to find from Table 1 that the specific surface areas of the two materials are quite different. It is apparent that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs specific surface area and pore volume of the composite material are improved compared with α-Fe2O3, which may be due to the small size of CQDs, and it will improve the specific surface area of the material. It can be seen from the pore size distribution that the pore size distribution of α-Fe2O3 is mainly between 60 and 100 nm, γ-Fe2O3 is mainly between 30 and 40 nm, and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs is mainly between 5 and 30 nm. The pore size decreases obviously, which may be due to the formation of some quantum dots into the γ-Fe2O3 pores during the high-temperature hydrothermal process. Overall, a larger specific surface area can increase the contact area between the material and the reaction molecules, thus improving the catalytic efficiency of photo-Fenton.

The degradation properties of the synthesized photo-Fenton catalytic materials were evaluated by removing various simulated dye pollutants. Using RhB as the main pollutant, the treatment effect of RhB under distinct reactional situations was observed in Fig. 3 . In the absence of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst, the RhB concentration was almost unchanged in only H2O2 and visible light systems. A similar situation is in the CQDs/Vis. system, indicating the need for a catalyst. In the absence of H2O2 and visible light, α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs resulted in only a slight reduction in RhB and should be attributed to physical absorption. Compared with pure α-Fe2O3, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs degradation rates of RhB have a 20% performance improvement, owing to the decomposition of H2O2 and the adsorption of carbon quantum dots (Yin et al., 2019). Fig. 3b is the reaction rate diagram of a quasi-primary kinetic reaction, where the slope represents the reaction rate. It can be observed that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs increases the first-order rate constant of α-Fe2O3 by about 10 times. The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs can be observed that the degradation rate of RhB can achieve more than 90%, compared with the un-doped removal rate increased by more than 15%, indicating that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst has better visible light catalytic performance from Fig. 3b.

The specific results are presented in Fig. S1 following the optimization of the preparation conditions.The detailed analysis process were described in Text S4. Considering the important role of CQDs, Fig. 3c comprehensively compares the photo-Fenton catalytic performance of catalysts with different doping ratios under the visible light system of H2O2. The molar ratios of iron and carbon are (1:0.05, 1:0.2, 1:2, 1:4), which are denoted as γ-Fe2O3/CQDsx for convenience, where x is the number of moles of carbon. Compared with pure α-Fe2O3 catalyst, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst can significantly improve the catalytic performance, indicating that CQDs play a positive role. To compare the capacity differences of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalysts made with different Fe/C ratios, the first-order kinetic reaction rate constants are shown in Fig. 3c. The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs2 has the highest rate constant of 0.3157 min−1, which is 14 0.1, 10.46, 10.64, and 8.8 times higher than that of α-Fe2O3, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs0.05, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs0.2, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs4. It is shown that the supported CQDs can greatly enhance the degradation performance of the catalyst. This result indicates that its activity is significantly enhanced and indicates that CQDs promote the electron transfer efficiency between Fe2+ and Fe3+.

The dose of H2O2 is one of the available factors for evaluating the possible application of cost control, so the infection of different amounts of H2O2 on catalytic efficiency was explored, and the results are observed in Fig. 3d. It is scanned for different dosages of H2O2, the catalytic efficiency of the catalyst does not change much. In the case of a lower dosage, the degradation rate of the catalyst to degrade rhodamine b can still reach more than 90%. And this further demonstrates the excellent catalytic ability of the catalysts. However, a high concentration of H2O2 inhibits the catalyst rate, When the H2O2 concentration achieves a critical value, the produced •OH can be converted into less reactive •HO2 by the overdose of H2O2, thus reducing the Rhodamine B reduction (Zhuang et al., 2020).

Further explores the impression of the amount of catalyst on the degradation performance of the catalyst. With the catalyst, content increased from 0.1 to 0.5 g L−1, the first-order kinetic reaction rate constant increased from 0.0325 to 0.1559 min−1, and the optimum amount of catalyst was also explored. As the concentration of the catalyst is greater than 1 g L−1, the degradation rate of RhB is decreased, which may be owing to the reduced light penetration intensity required to generate electron-hole pairs (Liu et al., 2017). This situation shows •OH production thus reducing RhB processing efficiency Too low or too high catalyst dosage is not conducive to catalyst degradation of pollutants (Clarizia et al., 2017). The reason for the analysis may be that when the catalyst is used in small amounts, not enough Fe3+/Fe2+ can be produced to react with hydrogen peroxide, while the overdose is the opposite. Fig. 4 b observed the influence of the original concentration of RhB on the effect of catalyst degradation performance. When the RhB is 10 mg L−1, the first-order kinetics constant is best, but when the RhB initial concentration increases from 20 to 40 mg L−1, the first-order kinetics constant has reduced from 0.3056 to 0.0888 min−1, Since RhB covers the catalyst site, •OH generation is inhibited.

Fig. 4c explores the impression of the original pH on the performance of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs to degrade pollutants. The catalyst has excellent dye degradation performance within a wide pH scope of 3.1–7.1, especially when pH = 3.12 the efficiency of the catalyst is consistent with the previous reports (Wang et al., 2014; Du et al., 2020). However, the catalytic efficiency in the alkaline environment is compared to the acidic or slightly less acidic conditions. This indicates that the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalytic material can work in a wider pH range than traditional Fenton materials. To access the general applicability of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs, the elimination of various pollutants such as MB, MO, and RhB by γ-Fe2O3/CQDs was determined, as scanned in Fig. 4d. The first-order kinetics invariable of MB and RhB were 0.0377 and 0.3157min−1, respectively, while the first-order kinetics constant of MO was lower. However, it is obvious that the removal rates of all pollutants show satisfactory removal effects, and the removal rates are all over 80% in 60 min. The results show that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs have good versatility for the removal of recalcitrant contaminants.

In this work, a comprehensive comparison was made between the preparation of composite catalysts by CQDs doping and different methods reported in the literature, and the results are shown in Table 2 . The γ-Fe2O3/CQDs prepared in this study have the advantages of being green, easy to process, less hydro-gen peroxide, wide visible light response, and a wider pH range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of theγ-Fe2O3/CQDs and magnetic recovery performance
ce:section.ce:para: For exploring the stability of the photo-Fenton structure of the catalyst and catalytic activity during long-term use, γ-Fe2O3/CQDs were evaluated by repeated degradation experiments. Fig. 5 a showed that the activity of the catalyst did not decrease significantly after four reiterant experiments. During the first three cycles of the assay, the efficiency of RhB degradation remained above 90% for 60 min. In the fourth experiment, the degradation efficiency was maintained at 80%, suggesting that the catalyst maintained excellent stability in the long time of use. The iron ion concentration remained stable at around 0.24 g/L after four cycles of experiments, indicating that the catalyst has a significant contribution to the degradation of pollutants. In addition, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs catalyst prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of glucose and catalyst effectively reduced part of ferric iron, making the prepared catalyst magnetic, and better solving the disadvantage of difficult recovery of nano-catalysts. The magnetization hysteresis loops of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs were measured at room temperature with an obtained saturation magnetization of 0.455 emu/g (Fig. 5b), and γ-Fe2O3/CQDs could be separated rapidly from the solution after exposure to an external magnetic field (Fig. 5b inset). The results show that γ-Fe2O3/CQDs are magnetic. This property can be used to recover the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs composite for recycling.

In general, the main active species in the Fenton procedure is •OH. To explore γ-Fe2O3/CQDs the main work active species involved in the dye treatment process, Tertiary butyl alcohol (TPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), EDTA-2Na, and NaN3 were selected as •OH, •O2−, holes (h +), and 1O2 radical scavenger in the photo-Fenton system. In the free radical quenching experiment, the active species involved in this process are identified by examining the impact of varying concentrations of capture agents on pollutant degradation. Details are presented as illustrated in Fig. S2.Under the same experimental conditions, 0.5 mM–2 mM of BQ was added to Fig. S2a, and the results showed that the degradation rate of Rhodamine b remained above 95% after a reaction time of 1 h. At the same time, the rate reaction graph in Fig. S2b also showed that the reaction rate did not significantly decrease after the addition of BQ, indicating that •O2−was not the main active substance in the degradation process. Under the same experimental conditions, the addition of 0.5 mM–2 mM EDTA-2Na to Fig. S2c showed that the degradation rate of Rhodamine b remained above 95% at a reaction time of 1 h. At the same time, the rate reaction graph in Fig. S2d also showed that the reaction rate did not significantly decrease after the addition of EDTA-2Na, indicating that h+ was not the main active substance in the degradation process. Under the same experimental conditions, 0.5 mM–2 mM of TBA was added to Fig. S2e. The results showed that at a reaction time of 1 h, the degradation rate of Rhodamine b decreased from 95% to 70% with the increase of TBA concentration. At the same time, the rate reaction graph in Fig. S2f also showed a significant decrease in the reaction rate after the addition of TBA, indicating that • OH plays an important role in the degradation process. Similarly, under the same experimental conditions, adding 0.5 mM–2 mM of NaN3 resulted in a significant decrease in the degradation rate of Rhodamine b to around 40%, and a significant decrease in the reaction rate constant. Therefore, 1O2 plays an important role in this degradation process, as shown in Figs. S2g and S2h.

It can be seen from Fig. S7b that apart from TBA and NaN3 has a certain inhibitory effect on the photo-Fenton performance, and the first-order kinetics constant was only 55%, and 20%, respectively. Among them, BQ and EDTA-2Na have no inhibitory effect on the photo-Fenton process.

To verify the specific mechanism of the reaction course more clearly, the singlet oxygen (1O2) and the signals of hydroxyl radical (•OH) were recorded by an EPR spectrometer, and TEMP and DMPO were used as trapping agents (Shanmugam et al., 2015). The specific experimental results are interpreted in Fig. S7c and Fig. S7d. As shown, a specific quartet spectrum with peak height ratios of 1:2:2:1 and three iso-height peaks of 1:1:1 were also detected, representing characteristic peaks of •OH and 1O2 respectively. In the visible-light/catalyst/H2O2 system, the peak value significantly increased, indicating that more •OH and 1O2 active species were formed during the process.

The reaction solutions of RhB were analyzed using HPLC-MS after 0 min, 30 min, and 60 min, respectively. The detected samples' components were primarily RhB and its degradation products. Based on the active species involved in the reaction, Table S1 illustrates the most likely degradation products. From Table S1, it is evident that the degradation products are measured at m/z = 416, 394, 387, 359, 290, 270, 183, and 149. Based on the m/z values, Fig. 5d illustrates potential degradation pathways. There are two potential oxidation reactions of RhB, namely the disintegration of RhB chromophore structure and N-deethylation. The majority of N-deethylation processes commence with the generation of a nitrogen-based free radical, whereas the elimination of the chromophore structure of the dye results in the creation of a carbon-based free radical. The particular radical that is primarily formed - either nitrogen-centered or carbon-centered - is determined by the hydrolysis or deprotonation of the dye cationic radical.

The initial step involves the N-deethylation process, which involves a step-by-step degradation of RhB into N-deethylation intermediates. These intermediates include N, N-diethyl-n'-ethyl RhB (m/z 416), N′-ethyl-n-ethyl Rhodamine (m/z 387), and N-ethyl Rhodamine (m/z 359). Methyl RhB (m/z 290) is linked to the deethylation and decarboxylation of RhB. Following this, the chromophore of RhB undergoes a series of cleavages, resulting in the opening of the aromatic ring to generate small organic acids that ultimately undergo mineralization into CO2 and H2O. Regarding COD and TOC data, as shown in Fig. S8. From Fig. S8, it can be seen that the COD removal rate of the catalyst modified by quantum dots can reach about 70%, while the COD removal rate of the unmodified catalyst is only about 50%, and the same TOC removal rate can also reach about 50%. This further improves the safety performance of the catalyst.

According to the above experimental process and results, the possible mechanism of RhB degradation by γ-Fe2O3/CQDs was proposed in Fig. 5c and equations (5)–(16). A typical photo-Fenton reaction produces •OH (Eq (5), (6)) and (7) (Jin et al., 2017). Firstly, γ-Fe2O3 is excited to produce electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. Secondly, the photo-generated electron is activated from the ligand to the metal surface, and the Fe3+ acceptor electrons are reduced to Fe2+. The Fe (Ⅱ)3d orbital adsorbed by H2O2 on the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs surface partially overlaps with the sp3 hybridized orbital, weakening the O–O bond. This is beneficial for the decomposition of H2O2. And it can generate a large amount of active •OH, and promote the degradation of RhB. The consumption of H2O2 during Fe3+ reduction is reduced due to the participation of photo-generated electrons. Efficient Fe3+/Fe2+ cycling ensures sufficient Fe2+ to partake the photo-Fenton reactions (Eqs. (5)–(7)) (Jin et al., 2017). Because the CQDs are successfully applied to γ-Fe2O3 to attract electrons (Ma et al., 2018), as electron acceptors, CQDs receive a large number of photo-generated electrons and react with H2O2 adsorbed on the surface to generate •OH (Eq (8) and (11)), which inhibits the composition of electroporated pairs and electrons were moving rapidly between the Fe and H2O2(Ma et al., 2018). Thus it could enhance the degradation efficiency of rhodamine b. In addition, CQDs used as electron pools can restrain the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, leading to electron-hole pairs with adsorbed oxidant/reductant (usually O2−/•OH) (Wu et al., 2021a). This intermediate is the main source of the production of another active substance 1O2. The • O2− reacts with water to produce another active species of singlet oxygen (Eq. (12) to (13)). In addition, the combination of Fe3+ and O2 •-/•OH can also form 1O2 and participate in the degradation of dyes (Yang et al., 2019) (Eq. (14)). One π orbital of the excited state 1O2 is occupied by two electrons with opposite spins simultaneously, and the other π orbital has no electrons. This electronic structure endows 1O2 with strong oxidation and electrophilicity, which is of great help to the degradation of pollutants (Jiang et al., 2022). Anyway, in this process, hydroxyl radical and singlet oxygen are the main active groups of Rhodamine b degradation (Eq. (15) to (16)).

Fig. 5e shows the structural diagrams and free energy curves for the adsorption and dissociation of H2O2 on α-Fe2O3 (220) and γ-Fe2O3 (220) surfaces, respectively. Geometrically optimized H2O2 molecule initially adsorbed on the iron sites on both α-Fe2O3 (220) and γ-Fe2O3 (220) surfaces, and the adsorption on γ-Fe2O3 (220) is stronger than that on α-Fe2O3 (220). The H2O2 molecule was subsequently cleaved uniformly by the single iron site to form reactive hydroxyl radicals which still adsorbed on the iron sites, and the Gibbs free energy is −2.33 eV on α-Fe2O3 (220) while it is 0.08 eV on γ-Fe2O3 (220). However, the desorption of OH* from the α-Fe2O3 (220) surface requires an energy barrier of 0.54 eV, while it is energy favorable on the γ-Fe2O3 (220) surface. Therefore, the mechanism of H2O2 dissociation reaction on α-Fe2O3 (220) is different from that on γ-Fe2O3 (220). The potential determination step (PDS) on α-Fe2O3 (220) is the cleavage of H2O2 with an energy barrier of 0.54 eV, while it is the desorption of OH* on γ-Fe2O3 (220) with an energy barrier of only 0.08 eV. Thus it can be deemed that γ-Fe2O3 performs a much better catalytic activity for the dissociation of H2O2 than α-Fe2O3.

The CQDs were successfully doped into α-Fe2O3 by a simple, green, and economical one-pot method. In the presence of high-temperature hydrothermal and carbon quantum dots, the crystal form of α-Fe2O3was successfully changed to γ-Fe2O3, and a new material γ-Fe2O3/CQDs was prepared. By forming stable Fe–O–C bonds will be highly fragmented CQD fixed on the γ-Fe2O3, and then combined with visible light and H2O2 to form a new photo-Fenton reaction process to degrade MO, MB, and RhB dyes. The results exhibited that the degradation rate of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs is 14 times higher than that of conventional α-Fe2O3 even with an extremely low concentration of H2O2 of 0.049 mmol.L−1 and a much wider pH range of 3.1–7.1. These results are crucial for reducing operating costs, and the active species of •OH and 1O2 mainly contributed to the visible light photo-Fenton reaction of γ-Fe2O3/CQDs. According to the results of the DFT calculation, the structure of γ-Fe2O3 is more conducive to the desorption of hydroxyl radical than the photo-Fenton reaction. In addition, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs also showed good catalytic activity and reliability for four times reuse cycles, and the degradation efficiency was still as high as 80%. Last but not least, the γ-Fe2O3/CQDs own saturation magnetization of 0.455 emu/g, which benefits a handy recovery of the nano-catalyst and could cause no secondary pollution.
